title: SACRAMENTO

raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "The legislation AB 1853 seeks to develop or expand computer science education training for individuals seeking a teaching credential in California.",
    "Only 41% of California public high schools offer at least one computer science course.",
    "California ranks 42nd nationally in high schools offering computer science courses.",
    "Nationwide, computing occupations make up 58% of all projected new jobs in the science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) fields.",
    "The U.S. Labor Statistics’ Occupational Outlook Handbook projects that computer and information technology occupations will grow 13 percent from 2020 to 2030.",
    "California currently has approximately 68,000 open computing jobs with an average salary of $115,754.",
    "Expanding access to computer science education for K-12 students starts with a strong pipeline of teachers.",
    "Tackling educational inequity is a step in diversifying the tech industry at scale.",
    "Growing the K-12 computer science pre-service pipeline is a foundational strategy for bringing computer science to schools."
  ]
}
```
